figures.caption: Figure 1: How an agent evicts decides whether it finishes. Context tokens per generation on one AppWorld task (d194965_2) under four policies at a 2048-token budget. No-compression grows unbounded (∼12.6k tokens); FIFO and LLM compression (ACON) stay bounded but lose the load-bearing content. ACON does 49 llm + 8 compressor calls. LRE keeps the relevant turns verbatim with a few kilobyte CPU scorer and completes in 19 calls, 37% fewer than no-compression’s 30.

Figure 3: LRE: budgeted extractive eviction. The cap B applies to the older selected units S⋆; the recent window Rk (and the system prompt and task, omitted here) are appended without competing for B. Older units are added in decreasing value density pi/ci, skipping any that overflow. The two domains instantiate (ci, B,Rk) as in Table 1.

Figure 6: Gold recall versus retention budget on both benchmarks. On LoCoMo, LRE retains more gold evidence than every baseline at every budget; on LongMemEvalS LRE tracks just below content salience but reaches it at a higher budget.
Figure 7: LoCoMo evaluation pipeline for query-agnostic context eviction in LRE.
Figure 8: LongMemEvalS evaluation pipeline for session-level context eviction under query-dependent retrieval.

Table 6: Unified feature space used by LRE across agentic execution traces (Domain A: AppWorld) and conversational QA (Domain B: LoCoMo and LongMemEvalS). The model shares a small set of causal structural features across domains, while augmenting them with domain-specific lexical, conversational, or code-execution signals. All features are computed over prefix histories u≤i to ensure strict causality during training and inference. The feature set is constructed as a minimal causal representation of interaction structure, motivated by observed failure modes across domains.
Figure 9: AppWorld: agentic evaluation of LRE under token budget constraints and Pareto efficiency criteria.

Figure 10: Domain A (agent): shared eviction trigger; methods differ only in HANDLEOLDER. No-compression keeps everything (unbounded), FIFO drops the older steps, ACON replaces them with one abstractive LLM summary, and LRE keeps the highest learned-relevance-per-token steps verbatim.

Figure 11: Domain B (conversational): shared score-then-retain eviction, with each baseline’s per-unit score. agei is distance from the most recent unit; c̄U , ēU are the group TF-IDF / embedding centroids; query-aware references read the question and are upper bounds, never competitors.

Figure 12: Recovery of true TGC by replay ($0, no LLM call): replay_eval.py. A fresh environment resets to the initial state, so we replay the logged actions to reconstruct the agent’s final state before evaluating.

Table 11: The 14 AppWorld test_normal tasks (seed 1) solved by LRE alone: hidden unit-test pass counts (passed/total) per policy. LRE passes all tests on every task, while no-compression, FIFO, and ACON each leave at least one test failing.

Table 12: Downstream answer quality at a 20% retention budget. On LoCoMo (token-F1) the full context is best but token-heavy, and LRE leads the budget-constrained policies; on LongMemEvalS (LLM-judge) the full context is worst and the deployable policies cluster.

Table 13: Conversational serving cost and iso-recall budget saving. LRE does zero neural forward passes and scores resident units on a CPU, whereas the dense and LLMLingua-2 encoders pay one forward pass per unit (102–103× slower). At iso-recall (80% gold recall) LRE reaches the target at roughly half the budget of recency.

Figure 13: Downstream answer quality versus token cost under a 20% retention budget (Full context † = no eviction). The comparison set is minimal and deployment-oriented: recency is the production default, content salience the strongest non-neural query-blind competitor, and the LRE variants test whether proactive relevance transfers to QA; MemoryBank (ranking-equivalent to recency) and the neural salience baselines (dominated by content salience) are omitted.
